claim: Incomplete fix: AmbiguousMatchException still thrown when a new-hide with differing signature lives on an intermediate base (not the runtime type), since DeclaredOnly finds nothing and the FlattenHierarchy fallback collects two candidates.
BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy is a no-op for instance members here (only affects static members), so the second call is behaviorally identical to Public|Instance alone — misleading.
Both new GetProperty calls omit BindingFlags.Static present in the original default flags; a matching static property is now silently unresolved and cached as null.
No material performance issue — the added second GetProperty call is cache-miss-only, bounded by the static ConcurrentDictionary cache keyed by (type, field), off the lock-free hot path.

Fallback lookup still throws AmbiguousMatchException when the leaf inherits a mid-hierarchy shadowed property without redeclaring it; FlattenHierarchy is inert for instance members so the fallback equals Public|Instance name-only lookup.
No rationale captured for the two-stage lookup, and FlattenHierarchy is functionally inert and misleading — implies a wrong mental model of how inherited-property resolution works.

Test suite omits the one shape that still fails — leaf inherits a shadowed property without redeclaring it; all seven new tests resolve via the DeclaredOnly step and never exercise the ambiguous fallback.
The DeclaredOnly-first ordering is the correct instinct and genuinely fixes the common, highest-frequency shadowing case (leaf redeclares with new), which is what issue #27095 reported.
Good breadth of added tests across several shadowing shapes, including the property-caching interaction and the shadow-has-no-attributes case — meaningfully raise confidence for the covered shapes.
SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes correctly confirms the leaf declaration wins over an attributed base, which is the right resolution semantics.

Model inherits (does not redeclare) a property a base shadowed with new + differing signature -> DeclaredOnly null, FlattenHierarchy collects 2 candidates -> AmbiguousMatchException re-thrown, uncaught in OnFieldChanged; pre-existing crash shape, not newly introduced.

FlattenHierarchy is paired with Instance only here; the codebase's only two other FlattenHierarchy usages (ParameterBindingMethodCache.cs) always pair it with Static, since it only affects static-member lookup — the fallback call is functionally identical to Public|Instance alone.
All 7 new tests construct new FieldIdentifier(model, nameof(...)) directly instead of the sibling-canonical editContext.Field(nameof(...)) helper used elsewhere in the same file.
MatchesPropertyByExactName hardcodes the literal "OrderID" while every other reference to that property and the file's established convention uses nameof(DerivedModelWithHiddenProperty.OrderID).

FlattenHierarchy fallback can still throw AmbiguousMatchException when a base type shadows a property via new and the most-derived type does not redeclare it — raised by Youssef1313 in the PR's final thread, left unaddressed by the author.

Two-step lookup only prevents AmbiguousMatchException when the hidden property is redeclared directly on the model's exact runtime type; if hidden 2+ levels above and the leaf doesn't redeclare, the fallback reintroduces the same exception.

Two-step property lookup reads as redundant; the rationale (avoiding AmbiguousMatchException on new-hidden members) is undocumented at the call site, inviting a future 'simplification' that reintroduces the fixed crash; FlattenHierarchy is a comprehension trap.

SPEC_PARTIAL: the generalized requirement 'no AmbiguousMatchException for hidden members' from issue #27095 is only partially met — leaf inheriting (not redeclaring) an intermediate-hidden member still throws via the FlattenHierarchy fallback.

SPEC_UNCOVERED: both new GetProperty calls drop BindingFlags.Static from the original default flags; public static properties with validation attributes are now silently skipped, contradicting the PR's 'No breaking changes' claim.

Coverage gap: the leaf-does-not-redeclare shadow-in-base shape is untested; ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels masks it because DeepDerivedModel redeclares Tag itself, so step 2 (FlattenHierarchy) is never exercised against a shadowed hierarchy.
ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithPropertyCaching does not actually verify caching — assertions only check validation messages, so a cache hit and miss are indistinguishable; the test is misnamed.

Weak Assert.Empty guards in SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes and IgnoresStaticProperty — an empty result also holds if the property is never found at all, so neither test proves the intended mechanism.

MatchesPropertyByExactName is redundant with ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithoutAmbiguousMatchException and its name implies an inexact-match branch that doesn't exist (GetProperty is always exact, case-sensitive).

ValidatesInheritedPropertyFromBaseClass is not a regression guard for the shipped bug — BaseName was never ambiguous, so it passes on the original pre-fix code; it only guards the step-2 fallback for non-shadowed properties.

Unused fixture member MidLevelModelWithShadow.Tag is never validated as its own runtime type — it only serves as an inheritance hop, creating the illusion of multi-level shadow coverage without providing it.
The static _propertyInfoCache is shared across all tests with no reset between them; benign today but tests are not truly isolated and could become order-dependent as more tests are added.

Fallback still throws AmbiguousMatchException when the runtime type inherits a base-shadowed property without redeclaring it; corroborated by 6 reviewers, matching the exact #27095 stack trace; the last review comment flagging this was left unaddressed at merge.

BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy is inert for instance members and inconsistent with the codebase's own usage — the repo's only other FlattenHierarchy uses always pair it with Static; the flag reads as load-bearing but is a no-op.
Two-step lookup has no rationale comment; the two near-identical GetProperty calls look redundant and invite a 'simplification' that reintroduces the fixed crash.
Test suite omits the one shape that still fails; ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels masks the gap because MidLevelModelWithShadow.Tag is never exercised as its own runtime type.
Dropping BindingFlags.Static silently stops validating static properties, contradicting the PR's 'No breaking changes' checkbox.
New tests build new FieldIdentifier(model, nameof(...)) instead of the sibling-canonical editContext.Field(nameof(...)).
MatchesPropertyByExactName hardcodes "OrderID" where siblings use nameof(...).
ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithPropertyCaching asserts only messages (identical hit vs miss) so proves nothing about caching; misnamed.
Weak Assert.Empty guards (SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes, IgnoresStaticProperty) — "empty" also holds if the property is never found.
MatchesPropertyByExactName redundant with test #1; name implies an inexact-match branch that doesn't exist.
ValidatesInheritedPropertyFromBaseClass passes on the pre-fix code (never ambiguous); guards the step-2 fallback, not the ambiguity fix — worth a clarifying comment.
MidLevelModelWithShadow.Tag never exercised as its own type (tied to the coverage-gap finding).
Static _propertyInfoCache shared across tests with no reset; benign now, but tests aren't isolated and could become order-dependent.

performance-reviewer returned no findings — the added lookup is cache-miss-only and bounded by the model schema; the author's DeclaredOnly→FlattenHierarchy choice is the cheapest of the alternatives the reviewers weighed.
PR-body accuracy: the description claims a try/catch on AmbiguousMatchException, but the merged code uses none — stale narrative, not an in-code defect.

ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels overclaims coverage; DeepDerivedModel redeclares Tag on the leaf, so it hits the easy DeclaredOnly path and never exercises the genuinely dangerous intermediate-shadow-inherited topology.
IgnoresStaticProperty is tautological: Assert.Empty(GetValidationMessages()) passes under both the fixed code and the reverted (pre-fix) code, so it provides no regression protection.
MatchesPropertyByExactName duplicates tests 1/2 with no added coverage - the OrderID string literal vs nameof(...) resolve to the same string and same code path.
SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes and IgnoresStaticProperty use weak Assert.Empty-only assertions that are also satisfied by a broken implementation that resolves the property to null.
Confusing sequencing in ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithoutAmbiguousMatchException: first NotifyFieldChanged fires with no intervening assert before OrderID is changed and re-notified.
ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithPropertyCaching is a sound caching test with correct expected/actual logic for every value, though the boundary value 100 is never exercised.
ValidatesInheritedPropertyFromBaseClass correctly exercises the pass-2 fallback for a non-shadowed inherited property and is the one test that meaningfully covers the fallback path succeeding.

Fallback lookup still throws AmbiguousMatchException when a property is hidden at an intermediate level and the leaf type does not redeclare it, since DeclaredOnly only rescues the case where the query type itself redeclares the member.
Sibling call site DefaultClientValidationService.BuildMetadata has the identical hidden-member bug and was left unfixed by this PR.
FlattenHierarchy in the fallback is a no-op for the instance-only lookup and misrepresents intent, masking the residual ambiguity bug.
Dropping Static from the binding flags is a deliberate behavior change with no rationale recorded in code comments, only in a test.
SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes is correct behavior, not a regression - DeclaredOnly-first matches C# hiding semantics where the derived member is authoritative.
Non-hidden inherited-only and leaf-declared property cases are preserved correctly by the new two-pass lookup, modulo the Static change.
The extra reflection lookup under concurrency is benign since the cache write is idempotent.
MidLevelModelWithShadow is defined but never instantiated as a leaf model - not dead code, but its absence as a leaf is what leaves the intermediate-shadow topology untested.
The DeclaredOnly-first strategy correctly and cleanly resolves the common leaf-hiding case reported in the original bug, and can never be ambiguous for that case.
Good test breadth overall: leaf hiding, caching, inherited-only, deep multi-level hiding, unattributed shadow, and static exclusion form a strong matrix.
Caching semantics (null values cached, cache cleared on metadata update) are preserved unchanged by this PR.

Fallback GetProperty call still walks the full type hierarchy without DeclaredOnly, so AmbiguousMatchException reappears when a property is hidden with new at an intermediate level and the leaf does not redeclare it.
BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy is a documented no-op here since it only affects static-member search, misleadingly suggesting it causes hierarchy traversal.
DeclaredOnly restricting the first lookup correctly excludes fields, private/internal properties, and static members - no issue.
The extra reflection call only occurs on a cache miss and is not a meaningful performance concern.
Thread-safety of the unsynchronized cache write is unchanged pre-existing behavior, out of scope for this diff.

ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels test naming implies deep-hierarchy disambiguation but it is satisfied entirely by the DeclaredOnly path; folded into the primary finding as supporting evidence rather than filed separately.
SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes and MatchesPropertyByExactName verified consistent with the two-call lookup logic - no issue.
IgnoresStaticProperty confirmed both calls correctly exclude statics regardless of FlattenHierarchy's no-op status - no issue.
The two-pass reflection lookup has no in-code rationale for the AmbiguousMatchException it silently sidesteps; a maintainer could collapse the two calls back to one and reintroduce the fixed bug.
Static-property exclusion is not flagged as a knowledge-preservation gap since intent is captured by the IgnoresStaticProperty test and BindingFlags.Instance makes the exclusion self-evident.
The existing "public properties" comment is not misleading after the change - it addresses public-vs-nonpublic, which is orthogonal to the new instance-vs-static and DeclaredOnly behavior.
Potential residual AmbiguousMatchException for a leaf that inherits without redeclaring an intermediate-shadowed property is noted but deferred as out of this reviewer's scope.
The _propertyInfoCache null-caching comment and lock-free note remain unchanged and accurate - no knowledge loss.
BuildMetadata resolves a model's validatable property via the same single-pass pattern the PR just replaced elsewhere, leaving this sibling call site on the ambiguity-prone pattern.
The 7 new tests drop the Arrange/Act/Assert comment convention used by the first 6 pre-existing tests, but siblings already disagree among themselves so this is not reportable.
The 9 new nested test-model classes use bare class, matching TestModel but not the more recently-added private sealed class async models - existing siblings disagree so not reportable.

GetPropertiesIncludingInherited in MemberAssignment.cs already does hierarchy-aware DeclaredOnly walking but solves a different problem shape (collect all properties vs. resolve one named property), so it is not a drop-in canonical helper here.

Other GetProperty call sites across Components (cascading-value binding) also use the plain non-two-pass pattern, reinforcing that it is the wider Components norm rather than a targeted omission - not filed as separate findings.
All 9 new nested model classes are exercised by tests or serve as bases in an exercised inheritance chain - no unused/leftover scaffolding.
New error-message literal formats (e.g. OrderID:range) match the existing PropertyName:attrtype convention exactly - no drift.
orderIdIdentifier variable casing diverges from existing casing convention (intFrom1To100Identifier preserves internal casing) but is too weak a single instance to report.
The FlattenHierarchy fallback still throws AmbiguousMatchException for a property new-hidden at an intermediate base level but not redeclared on the runtime type - confirmed by an out-of-tree repro.
BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy is a no-op in the fallback and misleads the reader into thinking hierarchy flattening resolves the shadow/ambiguity problem, confirmed by repro.
Removing BindingFlags.Static is an intentional tightening, not a silent regression, since it is codified by the new IgnoresStaticProperty test.
DeclaredOnly on the exact runtime type can never throw AmbiguousMatchException since a single C# type cannot declare two same-named properties - the sound half of the fix, confirmed by repro.
The existing IL2080 trimming-warning suppression remains valid for the new GetProperty overload since DeclaredOnly/FlattenHierarchy don't change the required trimmer member types.
The ConcurrentDictionary no-need-to-lock comment remains valid; the added second lookup only marginally enlarges a benign redundant recompute under concurrency.

The PR description claims a try/catch-based fix ("added handling for AmbiguousMatchException... retried") but the code contains no try/catch, which is not a code defect but is materially relevant since the described approach would have covered the still-broken topology.

A model whose leaf type inherits (does not redeclare) a new-shadowed differing-type property triggers AmbiguousMatchException on every field change, an unhandled exception that tears down the Blazor circuit.
The same hidden-member model that server-side Forms validation now handles still throws AmbiguousMatchException in BuildMetadata, which uses the original unpatched flags.
Dropping Static from the flags is an intentional, tested improvement since DataAnnotations' TryValidateObject never validates statics anyway - no cascade or failure chain.
Pass 1 (DeclaredOnly) throwing is impossible since C# cannot declare two same-named properties on one type; only Pass 2 can throw.
The leaf-redeclares-shadow-without-attributes test case matches C# member-hiding semantics correctly and is not a bug.
A constructed Static+shadow interaction scenario fell apart as a failure case since the consequence is only a benign, self-contained silent non-validation.

FlattenHierarchy fallback still throws AmbiguousMatchException when a property is new-hidden at an intermediate level and the leaf type doesn't redeclare it - corroborated by 5 reviewers and empirically reproduced.
Twin client-validation path DefaultClientValidationService.BuildMetadata has the same latent bug and is left unfixed, creating a server/client validation divergence.
Test ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels overclaims coverage; the still-broken intermediate-shadow topology remains untested.
IgnoresStaticProperty is a weak/likely-tautological test that passes even with the fix reverted.
The two-pass lookup and dropped Static flag carry no in-code rationale, so a future refactor could silently reintroduce the fixed AmbiguousMatchException bug.
BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy is a no-op for this instance-only lookup and misleads the reader into thinking hierarchy traversal is deliberately/safely handled, masking finding #1.
MatchesPropertyByExactName is redundant with the ValidatesHiddenProperties tests #1/#2 and should be folded into a Theory.
ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithPropertyCaching never exercises the inclusive boundary value 100.
SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes and IgnoresStaticProperty assert only Assert.Empty, which many implementations including a broken one would also satisfy.
Dropping Static from the flags is a correct, intentional tightening since validating a static property through a per-instance FieldIdentifier is meaningless.
Pass 1 (DeclaredOnly) can never throw AmbiguousMatchException since a C# type cannot declare two same-named properties - the DeclaredOnly-first half of the fix is sound.
The _propertyInfoCache no-need-to-lock comment remains valid since it's a ConcurrentDictionary and the extra lookup only enlarges a benign idempotent recompute - not a threading defect.
The IL2080 trimming-suppression remains valid for the new GetProperty overload but should be revisited if the BaseType-walk fix from finding #1 is adopted.

The PR description claims a try/catch-based fix with retry, but the code contains no try/catch; not a code defect but materially relevant since the described approach would have covered the still-broken topology.

Confusing sequencing in ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithoutAmbiguousMatchException where the first NotifyFieldChanged fires before OrderID is changed with no intervening assert - clarity only, suppressed by the confidence gate.

Two-step lookup only resolves hidden members when the model's own runtime type declares the shadowing `new` property; if an intermediate ancestor hides the member and the model type doesn't redeclare it, DeclaredOnly returns null and the FlattenHierarchy fallback re-throws AmbiguousMatchException (spec requirement R4 partial).

The DeclaredOnly-first fallback only resolves AmbiguousMatchException when the model's most-derived runtime type declares the hiding `new` property; if the hide happens at a non-leaf ancestor, the FlattenHierarchy fallback can re-collect both signatures and reproduce the exact exception the PR is meant to fix.

Both GetProperty calls now omit BindingFlags.Static (present in the original default lookup), so public static properties that previously matched a FieldIdentifier by name are now silently skipped; intentional (locked by IgnoresStaticProperty test) but undocumented as a behavior change.

BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy only affects static member visibility; combined with Instance and no Static it is a no-op here, misleadingly suggesting it flattens the instance hierarchy when that behavior actually comes from omitting DeclaredOnly.

Reviewer Youssef1313's unresolved PR thread concern — FlattenHierarchy is a no-op for instance members and the fallback GetProperty call can still throw AmbiguousMatchException — was never addressed in code or tests.

ilonatommy's PR thread request to 'think through any other edge cases' was only partially satisfied — ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels doesn't exercise the actual vulnerable fallback code path since its leaf class redeclares the hidden property itself.

Validator confirms the Static-exclusion finding: the diff drops BindingFlags.Static with no comment recording the two-step DeclaredOnly/FlattenHierarchy rationale or the Static omission; corrects the finding's line to 370. Notes it is regression-tested (IgnoresStaticProperty), so 'silently' is slightly strong — a documentation/knowledge-loss issue, not a live bug.

Two-step lookup still throws AmbiguousMatchException for a 3-level hierarchy where the hide happens at the middle level and the leaf simply inherits it without redeclaring; uncaught at the sole caller OnFieldChanged, reproduces on every subsequent field change since nothing gets cached before the throw.

Dropping BindingFlags.Static is an undocumented, silent behavior change; a future maintainer has no way to know the omission was deliberate versus an oversight and could reintroduce Static, resurrecting part of the original ambiguity surface.
BindingFlags.FlattenHierarchy in the fallback call is a no-op for instance-only lookups and misleads readers about which flag is load-bearing for the hierarchy search.

ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels resolves entirely through the DeclaredOnly branch (leaf redeclares Tag), so despite its name it never exercises the FlattenHierarchy fallback branch.

For C:B:A where B hides A's property with a different-signature `new` member and C does not redeclare it, validating C's field hits the FlattenHierarchy fallback with two same-name/different-signature candidates and re-throws AmbiguousMatchException; uncached, so every subsequent NotifyFieldChanged for that field re-throws, tearing down the Blazor Server circuit on each keystroke.

Both new lookups silently drop the Static flag; a model with a validated public static property that was previously validated on field-change now resolves to null and is silently skipped — invalid data on such a field passes the field-level check unnoticed (fails open).

MatchesPropertyByExactName hardcodes new FieldIdentifier(model, "OrderID") instead of nameof(DerivedModelWithHiddenProperty.OrderID), unlike every other reference to this property in the same diff and pre-existing sibling tests, which all use nameof(...).

Three new derived/base test-model pairs declare the derived class before its base class, contrary to the base-then-derived convention used by sibling test-model hierarchies in DefaultClientValidationServiceTest.cs.
New attributed test-model properties split the validation attribute and the property declaration across two lines, whereas every pre-existing attributed property in this file keeps them on one line.

The two-step reflection lookup (DeclaredOnly then FlattenHierarchy) has no comment explaining the AmbiguousMatchException it works around; a future maintainer could 'simplify' it back to a single GetProperty(name) call, reintroducing issue #27095.

Static properties are silently no longer validated, with no note at the edit site that this is intentional; the intent is recorded only in a sibling test a maintainer editing this method may never open.

FlattenHierarchy is a no-op for instance properties in the fallback call and misleads the reader about how inherited properties are actually found (via the absence of DeclaredOnly, not FlattenHierarchy).

Validator confirms the headline finding: no test class is shaped as D:C:B:A where an intermediate level hides with a different signature and the leaf doesn't redeclare; corrects pre_existing to true after tracing git history — the pre-fix single-arg GetProperty(name) performed the same unrestricted hierarchy walk and would have thrown for this exact shape too.

None of the 7 new tests reach the FlattenHierarchy fallback in a way that could reproduce ambiguity — every hidden-member scenario redeclares the shadowed member directly on the model's own runtime type; a leaf that inherits an already-doubly-shadowed member without redeclaring would plausibly still throw AmbiguousMatchException.

SkipsValidationWhenDerivedShadowHasNoAttributes only asserts Assert.Empty(...), which is produced identically whether the unattributed derived shadow was correctly resolved or no property was resolved at all; the file's own established pattern (onValidationStateChangedCount) would distinguish these but isn't used here.

MatchesPropertyByExactName duplicates ValidatesHiddenPropertiesWithoutAmbiguousMatchException — same model, same field, same assertion — adding test count without additional regression-guard coverage.

ValidatesPropertyHiddenAtMultipleInheritanceLevels overstates what it verifies: DeepDerivedModel declares Tag directly, so DeclaredOnly resolves it in step 1 and the test never exercises the FlattenHierarchy fallback despite its name implying multi-level fallback coverage.

IgnoresStaticProperty conflates the hidden-member fix with an incidental static-exclusion behavior change, and skips the stronger onValidationStateChangedCount assertion pattern used by the sibling test.

The static _propertyInfoCache is never cleared between tests; three new tests use the identical key (DerivedModelWithHiddenProperty, "OrderID"), so only whichever runs first exercises the live resolution logic — the others hit a pre-warmed cache entry, reducing test independence.

The two-tier BindingFlags fix only prevents AmbiguousMatchException when the hiding property is declared on the leaf (runtime) model type itself; when the hide occurs at a non-leaf ancestor and a further-derived type doesn't redeclare it, the fallback still reproduces the original exception.

Consolidated: the fix resolves ambiguity only when the model's exact runtime type declares the hiding new property; when the new-hide occurs at a non-leaf ancestor and the leaf doesn't redeclare it, the FlattenHierarchy fallback re-collects both signatures and re-throws AmbiguousMatchException — matching the PR's own unresolved review thread (Youssef1313), found independently by 7 reviewers and confirmed by a validator.

Incomplete fix: intermediate-level shadow still throws AmbiguousMatchException (loud, not silent) and is untested -- DeclaredOnly only disambiguates when the shadow is redeclared on the exact runtime type; for a 3-level hierarchy where the leaf doesn't redeclare, the FlattenHierarchy fallback re-throws.

Intentional behavior change: static properties are now excluded from both lookup steps, whereas the old default GetProperty(name) included Static; defensible since it aligns with form-level validation, but is a silent behavior change for anyone with a Range-attributed static property.

FlattenHierarchy fallback still throws AmbiguousMatchException when a property is new-hidden at an intermediate hierarchy level above a runtime type that doesn't itself redeclare it; step 1 DeclaredOnly returns null and step 2 reintroduces the exact ambiguity; no test covers a non-redeclaring derived leaf.

The same 'resolve validatable property by field name' contract is implemented divergently: DefaultClientValidationService.BuildMetadata uses a plain GetProperty without DeclaredOnly and still throws on the same new-hidden models; the existing canonical helper MemberAssignment.GetPropertiesIncludingInherited is not reused by either Forms path.
Positive: preferring the leaf-declared shadow (DeclaredOnly first) is semantically correct, matching the property model.Field member access actually binds to.
